key_insights.insight: Quality of sex is more important than quantity, and improving the quality can lead to an increase in quantity.
key_insights.quote: I would say quality generally, more so than quantity. Quantity you can fix when you fix quality.

key_insights.insight: Many people, especially women, dissociate during sex as a protective mechanism against emotional or physical pain, hindering true connection and pleasure.
key_insights.quote: It's where you kind of, you just check out. You just, you go through the motions physically,but you're not emotionally there. It's protection against being hurt again.

key_insights.insight: Lack of knowledge about sexuality creates fear, which is the enemy of pleasure; education and open communication are essential for better sex.

key_insights.insight: Couples should practice 'radical honesty' to address sexual issues, communicating boundaries, desires, and needs without sugarcoating or withholding.

key_insights.insight: Testosterone and estrogen influence body image perceptions, with men often overestimating their attractiveness and women underestimating theirs.

key_insights.insight: Safety and variety are both key to desire.

memorable_stories: Susan's story of her own journey to sexual awakening in her 40s after years of sexless marriage and childhood trauma, highlighting the transformative power of education and communication.
The story of Tim's affair and their daughter's question, 'But will you still be my daddy?' which prompted Susan to reconsider and work on fixing their marriage.
Susan's description of her first expanded orgasm, which felt like 'touching Source' and connecting with God, Gaia, and all that connects us.'
